old_claim.note: Bronze Casting Molds provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Early struck-coin dies were carved into bronze or possibly iron blocks, so bronze working is the nearer material/tooling background than bronze casting molds.

source_shape.source_locator: The Minting section describes handmade Greek coins and says obverse and reverse designs were carved into bronze or possibly iron blocks called dies.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: For struck Greek coinage, the die was a carved metal block, while a mold was only part of blank or flan preparation.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The replacement narrows the prerequisite to the material-working skill for the die itself; it prevents the coin-die node from being treated as a cast-coin or blank-casting technology.

ontology_before: Coin Die Engraving depended on bronze casting molds, which blurred hand-engraved struck-coin dies with molds used for casting blanks or separate cast-coin traditions.
ontology_after: Coin Die Engraving is scoped to hand-engraved dies and punches for struck coinage; bronze working carries the die-material/tooling context, while mold use stays outside the node scope.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: coin_die_engraving now depends on bronze_working and no longer directly depends on bronze_casting_molds.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to cast coinage or flan casting rather than hand-engraved struck-coin dies.
A stronger ancient numismatic source shows the earliest scoped dies were not bronze or iron metal tooling.
A dedicated coin_die_metallurgy or die_sinking node is added and should replace the broader bronze_working prerequisite.
short_reason: Coin dies are carved metal tooling; bronze casting molds were a misleading blank-casting edge.
